coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots from Guar Gum: Synthesis, characterization and preliminary in vivo application in plant cells
coredata.dc:creator: Bajpai, S.K.
D'Souza, A.
Suhail, Basharat
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, Guar Gum (GG) has been used as precursor to prepare carbon dots (CD) via microwave assisted synthesis. The carbon dots, as synthesized, were characterized by various analytical techniques such as Fourier Transformation Infra-Red (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) analysis, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and Fluorescent Microscopy (FM) etc. The TEM analysis revealed that 40% of the particles had diameter in the range of 20–30 nm while 15% had their size less than 10 nm. Zeta potential was found to be −3.65 mV, thus indicating presence of slight negatively charged surface. Finally, in a preliminary investigation, impregnation of CDs was observed in Hisbiscus Rosa sinensis leaf, as viewed through fluorescence microscope.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Guar Gum
Plant cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Insertion of carbon dots in plant tissue

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart showing protocol for the formation of carbon dots.
Passage of laser beam through the pale yellow solution of CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Proposed reaction involved in CDs formation from Guar Gum.
Integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance plot of C-dots and quinine sulphate.
UV spectrum of aqueous solution of CDs.
TEM image of carbon dots at 100 nm bar scale.
TEM image of carbon dots at 50 nm bar scale.
Fluorescence microscopic images with (a) blue emission under UV-filter, (b) red emission under green filter and (c) green emission under the red filter.
Fluorescence within the cells due to absorption of carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots from Guar Gum: Synthesis, characterization and preliminary in vivo application in plant cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Govt. Model Science College, Jabalpur, M.P. 482001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, St. Aloysius College, Jabalpur, M.P. 482001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, Guar Gum (GG) has been used as precursor to prepare carbon dots (CD) via microwave assisted synthesis. The carbon dots, as synthesized, were characterized by various analytical techniques such as Fourier Transformation Infra-Red (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) analysis, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and Fluorescent Microscopy (FM) etc. The TEM analysis revealed that 40% of the particles had diameter in the range of 20–30 nm while 15% had their size less than 10 nm. Zeta potential was found to be −3.65 mV, thus indicating presence of slight negatively charged surface. Finally, in a preliminary investigation, impregnation of CDs was observed in Hisbiscus Rosa sinensis leaf, as viewed through fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been acknowledged as discrete, quasispherical particles with their diameter below 10 nm [1]. They generally possess a sp2 conjugated core and contain suitable oxygen content in the form of multiple oxygen-containing species represented by carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aldehyde groups. In the last decade, carbon dots (CD) have emerged out as a new class of nano materials that are derived from precursors having enough carbon content within their molecular formula [2]. CDs may be regarded as carbon nanoparticles with extremely smaller size (less than 10 nm). They possess a number of remarkable properties such as fair hydrophilic nature, excellent biocompatibility [3], cell permeability, photo stability etc. [4–6]. In addition, presence of various functional groups (e.g. amino, hydroxyls, carboxyl) on the surface of CDs makes it susceptible to chemical modifications via various synthetic routes to enable them to attach covalently with different chemotherapeutic and targeting agents [7]. One of the most exciting and significant properties of carbon dots is their photo-luminescent (PL) emission, caused by their exposure to UV-radiation [8]. It is believed that the fluorescence property of CDs mainly exists due to various radiative electronic transitions that occur within or between the various functional groups present on the surface of the carbon dots [9]. It is also reported that PL emission is not much dependent on the size of the carbon dots, but is mainly governed by the nature of the functional groups that are locally present on their surface [10].

Due to the presence of various functionalities on the surface and their photo-luminescent property, carbon dots been exploited for a number of biomedical applications such as cell imaging and sensing [11,12], targeted drug delivery [13], in cancer theranostics [14], for screening the purine metabolic disorders in human fluid [15], drug delivery [16] etc. In addition, the PL property of carbon dots has also been employed in various analytical applications such as for the detection of vitamins [17], determination of pesticides [18], sensing of lead and pyrophosphate [19], determination of pollutants [20] etc. Moreover, they show non-blinking fluorescence emission, and they are readily soluble in water with the major advantage of comprising non-toxic elements with a non-toxic response. Indeed, this makes them a particularly useful, promising bio-analytical tool [21]. Luminescent Carbon dots may also be used to track biological processes that take place inside the cells. They are less toxic than similar alternatives and therefore more suitable to be used in live biological systems [22].

Polysaccharides, due to their abundance, low cost, presence of excessive functional groups and excellent biocompatibility, can serve as an excellent carbon rich source to synthesize carbon dots. In this study Guar Gum (GG) has been employed as a resource material to prepare carbon dots. A thorough literature survey reveals that GG has not been employed for this purpose till date. Guar Gum is a polysaccharide composed of the sugars galactose and mannose. The backbone is a linear chain of β 1,4-linked mannose residues to which galactose residues are 1,6-linked at every second mannose, forming short side-branches. The backbone is a linear chain of β 1,4-linked mannose residues to which galactose residues are 1,6-linked at every second mannose, forming short side-branches [23]. In this study, CDs have been prepared from GG using microwaves exposure and their complete characterization has been done using various analytical techniques. In addition, a preliminary investigation is made to see possible absorption of Cds in gard cells present in the leaves of Hisbiscus Rosa sinensis. It is noteworthy here that Guar Gum has been employed for the first time as carbon resource material to prepare carbon dots. In addition, insertion of carbon dots into plant leaf by direct immersion of leaf in CDs solution is relatively new approach. Because, in most of the studies reported, the plant roots are immersed in aqueous solution of carbon dots which translocate into the plant system and are distributed.

Guar Gum (GG; molecular mass 1.2 × 105 Da) was obtained from Hi Media Chemicals, Mumbai, India and was used with no further modification. The reference material quinine sulfate was obtained from E. Merck Chemicals, Mumbai, India. The double distilled water was used throughout the investigations.

Carbon dots were prepared by microwave assisted synthesis as per procedure provided by Milosavljevic et al. [24] with a little modification. In brief, 0.2 g of GG was dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water under normal stirring for 2 h to ensure its complete dissolution. Now, the solution was placed in the microwave oven (LG, model No. CE1041DFB, USA) and heated till the volume decreased to one fifth along with the color change from transparent to yellowish-red. The optimization of the reaction time in the microwave-assisted heating was also performed. The results indicated that it took almost 25–30 min to prepare carbon dots. It is worth mentioning here that the solution was exposed to microwaves only for 30 s and then allowed to stand for 5 min to avoid overheating. In all, five to six exposures were required for synthesis of CDs. Finally, the solution was cooled down to room temperature and then 30 mL of distilled water was added under moderate stirring. The brownish-yellow dark solution was centrifuged (Remi, India) for 15 min with revolution speed of 50,000 rpm. The pale yellow supernatant was collected in test tubes, covered with aluminum foils and kept in dark chamber for further use.

In order to determine the Quantum Yield (QY) of carbon dots synthesis, standard Quinine sulphate (QS) solution was prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 and its absorbance was measured at 340 nm [25]. In brief, four solutions of QS and carbon dots with varying concentrations were prepared and due care was taken that their absorbance was less than 0.1. Their fluorescence spectra were recorded at the same excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Now, plots were obtained between the integrated photoluminescence intensities and corresponding absorbance. The linear plots were used to calculate QY using the following expression: prepare the QY was calculated using the following formula:
(1)
QY sample
=
QY std
[
( m )
sample
/
( m )
std
]
(
η
sample
2
/
η
std
2
)
where sample and mstd are the slopes obtained from linear plots, ηsample is the refractive index of solvent used for carbon dots (i.e. water) and ηstd is the refractive index of the solvent used for dissolved QS.

Carbon dots were characterized by Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectroscopy using FTIR Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu8400, Japan), in the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1 at room temperature by KBr pelleting method.

The dry powder of CDs was also analyzed for crystalline nature using XRD analysis. These measurements were carried out on a Rikagu Diffractometer (Cu radiation = 0.1546 nm) running at 40 kV and 40 mA. Carbon dots powder was obtained by rotary evaporating the CDs colloid solution and subsequent drying the concentrated solution in a vacuum oven at 70 °C for 48 h.
The UV–Vis spectra of CDs solution were recorded using UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (EC, India). The UV measurements were carried out in the wavelength range of 200 nm–600 nm at room temperature.

The Zeta potential of CDs was obtained using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a Rikagu Diffractometer (Cu radiation = 0.1546 nm) running at 40 kV and 40 mA. The diffractogram was recorded in the range of 2θ from 10 to 50° at the speed rate of 2°/min. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80 kV; the TEM samples were made by placing a drop of nanoparticle ethanol suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The images were recorded at different magnifications.

In order to determine size of the nanoparticles and their size distribution, various TEM images were selected (some not shown in data) and size of a number of nanoparticles, selected randomly, was measured. Finally, their distribution curve was plotted. The size of each particle was measured via three modes, i.e. longitudinally, transversally and diagonally, and the average data were reported as mean ± standard deviation.

In order to image the carbon dots in Fluorescent microscope (Florescent Microscope, Thermo Fisher Scientific Model No. EVOS FLoid Cell Imaging Station) a drop of mounting medium was mounted on the microscope slide and coverslip was laid with the cells upside down on this drop. The specimen was pressed with the tweezers slightly so that the mounting medium was well distributed, without squeezing the sample. The volumes were chosen in a way that the coverslips were completely moistened. After focusing, viewing and zooming the (sample) slide in fluorescent microscope, the images were captured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insertion of carbon dots in plant tissue
ce:section.ce:para: In order to impregnate carbon dots into Hisbiscus Rosa sinensis leaf, leaves with around 5 cm of length were collected and their surface was sterilized with 0.1% Mercuric chloride solution. The sterilized leaves were transferred in the laminar air flow under aseptic conditions. Now, the leaves were cut into small pieces on a sterilized filter paper and transferred to a sterilized Petri dish filled with 0.1% CDs solution. After one hour, squash of the leaf tissues was prepared, washed with distill water and then observed under a fluorescent microscope at regular intervals.

Usually, the synthesis of CDs involves three main steps: carbonization, passivation, and surface functionalization. Carbonization is the process of converting organic precursor into its basic carbon containing residues. Passivation is a next step that is usually required, because pure carbon nanoparticles are usually non-fluorescent and the chemical treatment of the surface with different compounds is important step in production of CDs. This step introduces an insulating shell on the surface that minimizes the impact of surface-defect, trap sites, and direct quenching from the surrounding, which all will enhance the fluorescence emission. The functionalization is the last step necessary for production of effective carbon dots. Excellent optical properties of CQDs are arising from carboxyl or hydroxyl groups which are passivized by covering materials. In this work, Guar Gum, a bio-polymer, was used as a starting material. When its aqueous solution is allowed to be exposed to mild microwaves, the carbonization takes place, thus converting GG into residue containing basic carbon. We did not use the passivating agent, so it may be probable that residual polymer chains of Guar Gum produce a covering around the carbon nanoparticles and provide hydroxyl groups at the surface to render fluorescent property.

Finally, the reaction involved in the formation of carbon dots from Guar Gum may probably be worked out on the basis of the data available related with the microwave assisted thermal decomposition of Guar Gum. The reaction scheme, shown in Fig. 1c , suggests that as the aqueous solution of Guar Gum is exposed to microwaves, it begins to de-polymerize in to its constituent monomer units, namely mannose and galactose. On further exposure, carbonization takes place resulting in formation of carbon dots with –OH functionalities on their surface.

The linear plots, obtained between integrated photoluminescence intensities and corresponding absorbance, are shown in Fig. 2 . On substituting values of ‘m’ (respective slopes of linear plots) and QYstd (as 0.54) in Eq. (1), QY of carbon dots was calculated to be 7.5. This value is quite comparable to those reported by others using different types of carbon resources. For example, Wang et al. [26] reported a QY of about 7.3 for the CDs prepared by hydrothermal treatment of glucose as starting material. Moreover, the hydroxyl-functionalized carbon dots (H-CDs), obtained by mono esterification of ethylene glycol, had a QY of 13. This indicates that surface hydroxyls could increase the quantum yield of CDs. In the present work, it may be probable that hydroxyl groups from resource material Guar Gum may be present on the surface of carbon nanoparticles, thus enhancing the QY. There are several reports in which the QY of carbon dots was quite low. For example, Mitra et al. [27] and Tian et al. [28] reported QY of carbon dots as 1.94 and 0.43 respectively.

The FTIR spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 3 .In this spectrum, a broad peak appears at 3444 cm−1, probably due to the presence of hydrogen bonds, between surface hydroxyls of Guar Gum; 1728 cm−1 is due to CO stretching modes of carbonyl groups (CO) [17].The absorptions at 1145 cm−1 could be attributed to COC bonds. In addition, CH stretching vibrations (2908 cm−1) and deformation band of CH (1427 cm−1) also appear in the spectrum [29]. The presence of these groups in the carbon dots may be attributed to the fact that when Guar Gum solution is exposed to microwaves, the GG is converted into residues that contain basic carbon. Now these residual part, which contain hydroxyls, and carboxyl groups, are adhered on the surface of carbon nanoparticles and are responsible for the excellent photoluminescent properties. So, the respective frequencies appear in the FTIR spectrum of carbon dots also.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 4 , a typical absorption peak at ca. 239 nm was observed, which is assigned to the п to п* transition of CC bond [30], another absorption peak at 288 nm was also observed, which is attributed to n-п* transition resulting from CO along with a typical characteristic of fluorescent CDs [31].

In Fig. 5 , the broad diffraction peak around ∼22° corresponds to (1 0 0) plane of carbon, thus indicating the formation of amorphous graphitic carbon (sp2 hybridization). Similar results have also been obtained by Vandarkuzhali et al. [32] who also reported amorphous carbon dots, prepared from pseudo stem of banana plant.

In some studies, carbon dots with crystalline structure have also been observed. For example, in a study by Thambiraj and co-worker [33], the XRD of carbon dots, obtained from sugar cane bagasse, also exhibited a number of diffraction peaks at 11.4°, 20.6°, 22.8°, 42.3° and 45.7°, which corresponded to the reflections through (2 0 0), (9 3 1 1), (2 2 2), (5 3 3) and (7 1 1) planes respectively. It is also worth mentioning here that the method of fabrication of CDs play a major role in deciding the nature of the Carbon dots. For example, XRD analysis of CDs obtained from hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and poly(acrylamide) exhibit a broad low intensity diffraction peak at 20.66°, thus indicating amorphous carbon [19] as observed in the present work.

The results of TEM analysis are shown in Figs. 6a–6c
. It can be seen in Figs. 6a and 6b that particles have almost spherical geometry and their size varies up to 30 nm. It is also noteworthy that the particles are surrounded by fragments of oligosaccharides produced during the thermal treatment of Guar Gum solution via microwave radiations. In addition, some agglomerations are also observed. The TEM image with a bar scale of 5 nm is also shown in Fig. 6c.It can be seen that CDs exhibit discernible lattice fringes with inter-planar spacing of 0.35 nm which is close to the diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [34]. Ding et al. [35] have also reported a spacing of 0.32 nm for the carbon dots obtained from single walled carbon nano tubes.

The zeta potential measurement revealed a value of −3.65 mV, almost close to neutrality. Finally, we also determined particle size distribution curve from the data provided in various TEM images. For this, particle size of individual particles was determined from various images, selecting a good number of particles in random manner. The size distribution curve, as shown in Fig. 6d , reveals that around 40% particles exhibit a diameter range of 20 ± 0.76–30 ± 1.14 nm while 27% of particles have diameter in the range of 10 ± 0.62–20 ± 0.96 nm. In addition, 15% particles had diameter up to 10 ± 0.71 nm. This indicates that there are 15% of particles that can be claimed to match the size of carbon dots. It is also worth mentioning here the nature of the distribution curve indicates that it is far away from the normal distribution curve, which is usually obtained for nanoparticles. However, to some extent it appears to be a log-normal distribution curve. Here, percentage of particles with smaller size is quite large as compared to that of the particles with relatively bigger size (i. e. more than 30 nm). We can see that nearly 82% of the total particles have size less than 30 nm while 14% of the particles have size range between 30 and 60 nm. It indicates that there was greater tendency for the formation of smaller sized particles. Such type of log-normal distribution is also reported by others. For example, Odo [36] reported log-normal particle size distribution for powder obtained by 5 h milling of silicon wafers.
The photoluminescent emission spectra of N-carbon dots, at the excitation wave lengths of 260–340 nm, are shown in Fig. 7 .

It can be observed that almost no emission peak is observed till the excitation wavelength of 300 nm. However, beyond this excitation wavelength, a peak begins to appear in the spectrum. As the excitation wavelength is further increased, the peak becomes more prominent, showing an optimal value at 440 nm. It is noticeable here that for all the emission spectra displayed, the emission peak is found at 436 nm. It is also noteworthy that no shift in emission wavelength was observed with the increase in excitation wavelength. Similar results have also been reported elsewhere. For example, Fu et al. [37] prepared Arginine-modified carbon dots and reported luminescence peak around 470 nm at the excitation wavelength range of 320–560 nm. In another report [38], Graphene QDs were prepared by acidic oxidation and exfoliation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), and their photoluminescent emission spectra was observed at the excitation wavelength range of 310–370 nm, with strong emission peak at 560 nm for the excitation wavelength studied.

The fluorescence microscopic studies revealed the high florescence nature of CDs with different bandwidths. The particles were seen with blue emission under UV-filter, red emission under green filter and green emission under the red filter as shown in Fig. 8 a–c. Indeed, this wide range of fluorescence emission is highly advantageous and efficient compared to CDs with single green fluorescence [39]. The results suggests that the present CDs could be a good alternative to tradition dyes for fluorescent labelling, bio-imaging and bio-sensor applications [40].

The penetration of CDs into the plant system is inversely proportional to its size. Perhaps the large size of carbon particles are forbidden to enter the plant cell and therefore, get adsorbed on the surface of the leaf. The fluorescent carbon dots within the guard cells of the stomata of plant leaf are shown in Fig. 9 . The results suggest that some of the carbon dots, probably below 10 nm, were successfully absorbed in the cells to produce fluorescence.

It may be concluded from the above study that Guar Gum (GG), a plant polysaccharide, can be conveniently used as a starting material to prepare fluorescent carbon dots via microwave assisted synthesis. It was found that 55% particles had average diameter below 30 nm. According to reports, CDs are not suffering from photo-bleaching and photo-degradation and therefore demonstrate a great potential for cell imaging and labeling as a biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterial. The penetration of carbon dots into cells opens up new possibilities of their further action within the cells. It is probable that after reaching the plant cell, CDs may reach the nucleus, loosen the DNA structure, and increase the thionin gene expression to enhance the disease resisting power of plant. However, this speculation can only be materialized by a systemic and in- depth study of plant cells interaction with carbon dots.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to legal or ethical reasons.
